def: The smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical properties.
ex: Every {{atom}} consists of a nucleus surrounded by electrons.

def: A pure substance made of only one type of atom, not chemically divisible.
ex: Oxygen is an {{element}} with the chemical symbol O.

def: A positively charged subatomic particle found in the atomic nucleus.
ex: The number of {{proton}} defines which element an atom is.

def: An electrically neutral subatomic particle located in the atomic nucleus.
ex: A {{neutron}} has no charge but contributes to atomic mass.

def: A negatively charged subatomic particle that orbits the atomic nucleus.
ex: An {{electron}} occupies a shell around the nucleus.

def: The dense central core of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
ex: The {{nucleus}} holds nearly all of the atom's mass.

def: An atom of an element with the same protons but different neutrons.
ex: Carbon-14 is a radioactive {{isotope}} used in dating.

def: The number of protons in an atom's nucleus, defining the element.
ex: The {{atomic number}} of hydrogen is one.

def: The total number of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
ex: The {{mass number}} equals protons plus neutrons combined.

def: The average mass of an element's atoms measured in atomic mass units.
ex: The {{atomic mass}} of chlorine is about 35.5 units.

def: A chart arranging chemical elements by atomic number and recurring properties.
ex: Mendeleev created the first modern {{periodic table}}.

def: A horizontal row of elements in the periodic table.
ex: Elements in the same {{period}} have the same number of shells.

def: A vertical column of elements sharing similar chemical properties.
ex: Elements in a {{group}} have the same number of valence electrons.

def: An electron in the outermost shell that participates in chemical bonding.
ex: Sodium has one {{valence electron}} available for bonding.

def: The arrangement of electrons across an atom's shells and subshells.
ex: The {{electron configuration}} of neon is fully filled and stable.

def: An energy level around the nucleus where electrons are located.
ex: The first {{electron shell}} can hold up to two electrons.

def: An atom or molecule with a net electric charge from lost electrons.
ex: An {{ion}} forms when an atom gains or loses electrons.

def: A positively charged ion formed when an atom loses electrons.
ex: A sodium {{cation}} carries a single positive charge.

def: A negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains electrons.
ex: A chloride {{anion}} has gained one extra electron.

def: A group of two or more atoms bonded together chemically.
ex: A water {{molecule}} contains two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen.

def: A substance formed from two or more elements chemically combined.
ex: Water is a {{compound}} of hydrogen and oxygen.

def: An unreactive gaseous element with a full outer electron shell.
ex: Helium is a {{noble gas}} that rarely forms compounds.

def: A highly reactive Group 1 metal with one valence electron.
ex: Sodium is an {{alkali metal}} that reacts violently with water.

def: A reactive Group 17 nonmetal with seven valence electrons.
ex: Chlorine is a {{halogen}} used to disinfect water.

def: A central-block metal that can form ions of varying charges.
ex: Iron is a {{transition metal}} used in steelmaking.

def: A reactive Group 2 metal with two valence electrons.
ex: Calcium is an {{alkaline earth metal}} vital for bones.

def: An element with properties between those of metals and nonmetals.
ex: Silicon is a {{metalloid}} widely used in electronics.

def: An element that lacks metallic properties and tends to gain electrons.
ex: Sulfur is a {{nonmetal}} found in many minerals.

def: An element that conducts electricity and readily loses electrons.
ex: Copper is a {{metal}} that conducts heat efficiently.

def: A particle smaller than an atom, such as protons or electrons.
ex: A proton is one type of {{subatomic particle}}.

def: A region around the nucleus where an electron is likely found.
ex: Each {{orbital}} can hold a maximum of two electrons.

def: A fixed level of energy that an electron can occupy in an atom.
ex: Electrons jump to a higher {{energy level}} when they absorb energy.

def: A subdivision of an electron shell containing orbitals of one type.
ex: The p {{subshell}} can hold up to six electrons.

def: The distance from an atom's nucleus to its outermost electron shell.
ex: The {{atomic radius}} decreases across a period.

def: An atom's tendency to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond.
ex: Fluorine has the highest {{electronegativity}} of all elements.

def: The energy needed to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
ex: The {{ionization energy}} increases across a period.

def: A one- or two-letter abbreviation representing a chemical element.
ex: The {{chemical symbol}} for gold is Au.

def: A diagram showing how electrons fill an atom's orbitals with spins.
ex: The {{atomic orbital diagram}} uses arrows to show electron spin.

def: A proton or neutron, the particles making up the atomic nucleus.
ex: Each {{nucleon}} adds roughly one unit to the mass number.

def: The weighted average mass of an element's naturally occurring isotopes.
ex: The {{atomic weight}} accounts for isotope abundance.

def: The spontaneous emission of radiation from unstable atomic nuclei.
ex: Uranium exhibits strong {{radioactivity}} as it decays.

def: The process by which an unstable nucleus loses energy through radiation.
ex: During {{radioactive decay}}, a nucleus emits particles.

def: The time for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
ex: The {{half-life}} of carbon-14 is about 5,730 years.

def: A representation describing the structure and behavior of an atom.
ex: Bohr's {{atomic model}} showed electrons in fixed orbits.

def: The lowest energy state of an atom's electrons.
ex: In the {{ground state}}, electrons occupy the lowest available levels.

def: A higher energy state after an electron absorbs energy.
ex: An electron in an {{excited state}} soon releases a photon.

def: A unit of mass equal to one-twelfth of a carbon-12 atom.
ex: Mass is measured in the {{atomic mass unit}}, or amu.

def: The combining power of an element based on its electrons.
ex: The {{valency}} of oxygen is two in most compounds.

def: A molecule made of exactly two atoms bonded together.
ex: Oxygen exists naturally as a {{diatomic molecule}}, O2.

def: Consisting of a single atom, as with noble gases.
ex: Argon exists as a {{monatomic}} gas under normal conditions.

def: The region of probable electron positions surrounding the nucleus.
ex: The {{electron cloud}} represents where electrons are likely located.

def: The arrangement of protons, neutrons, and electrons within an atom.
ex: Understanding {{atomic structure}} explains chemical behavior.

def: Atoms tend to gain eight electrons in their outer shell.
ex: The {{octet rule}} explains why noble gases are stable.

def: The set of wavelengths emitted or absorbed by an element's atoms.
ex: Each element has a unique {{atomic spectrum}}.

def: The average mass of an element's atoms relative to carbon-12.
ex: The {{relative atomic mass}} of magnesium is 24.

def: The attraction that holds atoms together in molecules or compounds.
ex: Chemical reactions involve breaking and forming {{atomic bonding}}.

def: The scientific theory that matter is composed of discrete atoms.
ex: Dalton's {{atomic theory}} laid the foundation of modern chemistry.

def: A group of electrons at a similar distance from the nucleus.
ex: The outer {{shell}} determines an atom's reactivity.

def: The release of light when excited electrons return to lower levels.
ex: {{Atomic emission}} produces the colours seen in flame tests.

def: A test identifying metal ions by the colour they turn a flame.
ex: A {{flame test}} shows sodium as a bright yellow.

def: A charged group of two or more bonded atoms.
ex: The sulfate {{polyatomic ion}} contains sulfur and oxygen.

def: The nucleus together with the inner, non-valence electrons.
ex: The {{atomic core}} stays intact during most chemical reactions.

def: The periodic table region where the outer s subshell fills.
ex: Group 1 and 2 elements form the {{s-block}}.

def: The periodic table region where the outer p subshell fills.
ex: The {{p-block}} includes nonmetals, metalloids, and some metals.

def: The central periodic table region containing the transition metals.
ex: Transition metals occupy the {{d-block}} of the table.

def: The tendency of a substance to undergo chemical reactions.
ex: The {{reactivity}} of alkali metals increases down the group.

def: Chemically unreactive and unlikely to form compounds.
ex: Noble gases are {{inert}} because of their full shells.

def: The repeating pattern of properties across the periodic table.
ex: {{Periodicity}} explains trends in atomic size and reactivity.

def: The net electric charge an atom carries after gaining or losing electrons.
ex: A neutral atom has an {{atomic charge}} of zero.

def: One of the fifteen rare-earth elements filling the 4f subshell.
ex: Cerium is a {{lanthanide}} used in catalysts.

def: One of the radioactive elements filling the 5f subshell.
ex: Uranium is an {{actinide}} used as nuclear fuel.

def: A process in which substances are converted into different substances with new properties.
ex: The {{chemical reaction}} between the two solutions produced a colour change.

def: A substance that takes part in and is changed by a chemical reaction.
ex: Each {{reactant}} is written on the left side of the equation.

def: A substance that is formed as a result of a chemical reaction.
ex: Water is a {{product}} of the combustion of hydrogen.

def: A chemical bond formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons.
ex: A {{covalent bond}} holds the two hydrogen atoms together in a molecule.

def: A bond formed by the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.
ex: An {{ionic bond}} forms when sodium transfers an electron to chlorine.

def: The attraction between metal ions and a shared sea of delocalised electrons.
ex: The {{metallic bond}} explains why copper conducts electricity so well.

def: A weak attraction between hydrogen and a highly electronegative atom nearby.
ex: A {{hydrogen bond}} gives water its unusually high boiling point.

def: A lasting attraction between atoms that enables the formation of compounds.
ex: Breaking a {{chemical bond}} usually requires an input of energy.

def: The loss of electrons or an increase in oxidation state by a substance.
ex: The {{oxidation}} of iron in damp air produces rust.

def: The gain of electrons or a decrease in oxidation state by a substance.
ex: The {{reduction}} of copper oxide leaves behind pure copper metal.

def: A reaction in which oxidation and reduction occur at the same time.
ex: A {{redox reaction}} transfers electrons from one reactant to another.

def: A substance that gains electrons and causes another substance to be oxidised.
ex: Chlorine acts as a powerful {{oxidising agent}} in this reaction.

def: A substance that donates electrons and causes another substance to be reduced.
ex: Carbon serves as a {{reducing agent}} when extracting metals from ores.

def: A substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up itself.
ex: A {{catalyst}} lowers the activation energy needed for the reaction.

def: The increase in the rate of a reaction caused by a catalyst.
ex: Enzyme {{catalysis}} makes biochemical reactions occur at body temperature.

def: A state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal.
ex: At {{chemical equilibrium}} the concentrations of reactants stay constant.

def: A substance that donates hydrogen ions and has a pH below seven.
ex: A strong {{acid}} fully dissociates when dissolved in water.

def: A substance that accepts hydrogen ions and neutralises acids.
ex: A {{base}} reacts with an acid to form a salt and water.

def: A soluble base that dissolves in water to give hydroxide ions.
ex: Sodium hydroxide is a common {{alkali}} used in laboratories.

def: A scale measuring how acidic or alkaline a solution is.
ex: The {{pH}} of pure water is seven at room temperature.

def: An ionic compound formed when an acid reacts with a base.
ex: The neutralisation produced a {{salt}} and some water.

def: An insoluble solid that forms and settles out of a solution.
ex: Mixing the two solutions produced a white {{precipitate}}.

def: A reaction that releases energy, usually as heat, to the surroundings.
ex: Combustion is an {{exothermic reaction}} that gives out heat and light.

def: A reaction that absorbs energy, usually heat, from its surroundings.
ex: Photosynthesis is an {{endothermic reaction}} that stores energy from sunlight.

def: The SI unit for the amount of a chemical substance.
ex: One {{mole}} of any substance contains Avogadro's number of particles.

def: The calculation of quantities of reactants and products in reactions.
ex: Using {{stoichiometry}}, we found the exact mass of product formed.

def: The minimum energy needed for a chemical reaction to begin.
ex: A catalyst reduces the {{activation energy}} required for the reaction.

def: How quickly reactants are converted into products over time.
ex: Raising the temperature increases the {{reaction rate}} noticeably.

def: The reaction of an acid with a base to form salt and water.
ex: The {{neutralisation}} of the acid raised the solution's pH to seven.

def: Using an electric current to break down a compound into elements.
ex: The {{electrolysis}} of water produces hydrogen and oxygen gases.

def: A reaction in which a substance burns rapidly in oxygen.
ex: Complete {{combustion}} of methane yields carbon dioxide and water.

def: A reaction in which two or more substances combine into one product.
ex: A {{synthesis reaction}} joins hydrogen and oxygen to make water.

def: A reaction in which one compound breaks down into simpler substances.
ex: The {{decomposition reaction}} of limestone releases carbon dioxide when heated.

def: A reaction where a more reactive element takes the place of another.
ex: In this {{displacement reaction}}, zinc pushes copper out of solution.

def: A symbolic representation of a chemical reaction using formulae and symbols.
ex: The balanced {{chemical equation}} shows equal atoms on both sides.

def: The amount of a substance present in a given volume of solution.
ex: Increasing the {{concentration}} of the acid speeds up the reaction.

def: A homogeneous mixture of a solute dissolved evenly in a solvent.
ex: The salt dissolved to form a clear {{solution}}.

def: The substance that is dissolved in a solvent to form a solution.
ex: In salt water, salt is the {{solute}} and water the solvent.

def: The substance, usually a liquid, in which a solute dissolves.
ex: Water is the most common {{solvent}} used in chemistry.

def: A technique to find a solution's concentration by controlled reaction.
ex: The {{titration}} showed that the acid needed twenty millilitres of alkali.

def: A substance that changes colour to show acidity or alkalinity.
ex: The {{indicator}} turned pink in the alkaline solution.

def: The splitting of a compound into ions when dissolved in water.
ex: Complete {{dissociation}} of the acid releases many hydrogen ions.

def: The gradual destruction of metal by chemical reaction with its environment.
ex: {{Corrosion}} weakens iron structures exposed to moisture and air.

def: A reaction in which water breaks a chemical bond in a compound.
ex: The {{hydrolysis}} of the ester produced an acid and an alcohol.

def: The joining of many small monomers into a long polymer chain.
ex: The {{polymerisation}} of ethene produces the plastic polythene.

def: A small molecule that can join with others to form a polymer.
ex: Each {{monomer}} links together to build a long chain.

def: A large molecule built from many repeating smaller monomer units.
ex: A {{polymer}} such as nylon is made from many monomers.

def: A substance that conducts electricity when molten or dissolved in water.
ex: The {{electrolyte}} allows ions to move and carry current.

def: A conductor through which current enters or leaves an electrolyte.
ex: Gas bubbles formed at the negative {{electrode}} during electrolysis.

def: The positive electrode where oxidation occurs during electrolysis.
ex: Oxygen gas is released at the {{anode}} during electrolysis of water.

def: The negative electrode where reduction occurs during electrolysis.
ex: Metal ions gain electrons at the {{cathode}} and are deposited.

def: A reaction that can proceed in both forward and backward directions.
ex: A {{reversible reaction}} can reach equilibrium under closed conditions.

def: The mass of one mole of a substance in grams.
ex: The {{molar mass}} of water is eighteen grams per mole.

def: The amount of product obtained from a chemical reaction.
ex: The reaction gave a high {{yield}} of pure crystals.

def: The energy needed to break one mole of a particular bond.
ex: A high {{bond energy}} means the bond is very strong.

def: A substance that slows down or stops a chemical reaction.
ex: An {{inhibitor}} is added to prevent the metal from corroding.

def: Describing a solution in which the solvent is water.
ex: The reactants were mixed as {{aqueous}} solutions in a beaker.

def: Able to be dissolved, especially in water or another solvent.
ex: Sodium chloride is highly {{soluble}} in cold water.

def: Unable to be dissolved in a particular solvent such as water.
ex: The {{insoluble}} solid was removed from the mixture by filtration.

def: Capable of destroying or damaging materials through chemical action.
ex: Concentrated sulfuric acid is extremely {{corrosive}} to skin.

def: To mix a solid into a liquid so it disperses evenly.
ex: Stir the powder until the crystals completely {{dissolve}}.

def: To undergo a chemical change when combined with another substance.
ex: Sodium will {{react}} violently with water to form hydrogen.

def: To adjust an equation so both sides have equal atoms.
ex: You must {{balance}} the equation before doing any calculations.

def: To speed up a chemical reaction by acting as a catalyst.
ex: Enzymes {{catalyse}} the reactions that keep living cells alive.

def: An organic compound made up entirely of hydrogen and carbon atoms.
ex: Crude oil is a complex mixture of many different {{hydrocarbon}} molecules.

def: A saturated hydrocarbon with single bonds and the formula CnH2n+2.
ex: Methane is the simplest {{alkane}} in the homologous series.

def: An unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbonвАУcarbon double bond.
ex: Ethene is the smallest {{alkene}} and is used to make plastics.

def: An unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbonвАУcarbon triple bond.
ex: Ethyne, commonly called acetylene, is the simplest {{alkyne}}.

def: A specific atom group giving a molecule its characteristic chemical properties.
ex: The hydroxyl {{functional group}} makes alcohols react in typical ways.

def: One of two compounds with identical formula but different atomic arrangement.
ex: Butane and isobutane are each an {{isomer}} of C4H10.

def: A chemical compound whose molecules are based on carbon atoms.
ex: Glucose is an {{organic compound}} found in living cells.

def: Separating liquids by boiling then condensing the resulting vapour.
ex: Fractional {{distillation}} separates crude oil into useful fractions.

def: A substance added to a system to cause or test a reaction.
ex: Add a few drops of the {{reagent}} to the test tube.

def: Separating solids from a liquid by passing it through a filter.
ex: Use {{filtration}} to remove the insoluble residue from the mixture.

def: The formation of solid crystals from a solution as solvent evaporates.
ex: Slow {{crystallisation}} produced large, well-formed copper sulfate crystals.

def: A technique separating a mixture by differing movement through a medium.
ex: Paper {{chromatography}} separated the ink into several coloured spots.

def: The change of a liquid into vapour below its boiling point.
ex: Leave the dish so {{evaporation}} concentrates the salt solution.

def: Easily evaporating at normal temperatures because of a low boiling point.
ex: Petrol is highly {{volatile}} and evaporates quickly in warm air.

def: An organic compound containing a hydroxyl group bonded to carbon.
ex: Ethanol is the {{alcohol}} found in fermented drinks.

def: An organic compound formed from an acid and an alcohol.
ex: Many fruity smells come from a volatile {{ester}}.

def: An organic acid containing the carboxyl functional group, вАУCOOH.
ex: Ethanoic acid is the {{carboxylic acid}} found in vinegar.

def: An organic compound with a carbonyl group at the chain's end.
ex: Methanal is the simplest {{aldehyde}} in the series.

def: An organic compound with a carbonyl group between two carbon atoms.
ex: Propanone, or acetone, is a common {{ketone}} solvent.

def: Describing a compound whose carbon atoms have only single bonds.
ex: Alkanes are {{saturated}} because they contain no double bonds.

def: Describing a compound containing carbonвАУcarbon double or triple bonds.
ex: Alkenes are {{unsaturated}} and decolourise bromine water.

def: A process in which substances change into different chemical substances.
ex: The chemical {{reaction}} released a large amount of heat.

def: A state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal.
ex: The reaction reaches dynamic {{equilibrium}} in the sealed flask.

def: Describing a reaction that releases energy to its surroundings.
ex: Combustion is an {{exothermic}} reaction that gives out heat.

def: Describing a reaction that absorbs energy from its surroundings.
ex: Photosynthesis is an {{endothermic}} process driven by light.

def: A gradual difference in solute concentration between two regions.
ex: Particles diffuse down a {{concentration gradient}} until evenly spread.

def: The spreading of particles from higher to lower concentration regions.
ex: The scent spread across the room through {{diffusion}}.

def: The change of a vapour into a liquid on cooling.
ex: {{Condensation}} forms droplets on the cold glass surface.

def: The direct change of a solid into a gas without melting.
ex: Solid carbon dioxide undergoes {{sublimation}} at room temperature.

def: The purified liquid collected after condensing vapour during distillation.
ex: The clear {{distillate}} was collected in a fresh flask.

def: The solid material left behind after filtration or evaporation.
ex: The insoluble {{residue}} remained on the filter paper.

def: The clear liquid that passes through a filter during filtration.
ex: The {{filtrate}} was collected in a clean beaker below.

def: The maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a solvent.
ex: The {{solubility}} of most salts increases with temperature.

def: Pure water produced by distillation, free of dissolved impurities.
ex: Rinse the apparatus with {{distilled water}} before use.

def: The вАУOH functional group characteristic of alcohols and many compounds.
ex: Every alcohol contains at least one {{hydroxyl group}}.

def: A family of compounds sharing a formula pattern and similar properties.
ex: The alkanes form a {{homologous series}} differing by CH2.

def: Breaking large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more useful ones.
ex: Catalytic {{cracking}} converts heavy oils into petrol and alkenes.

def: The breakdown of sugar by yeast producing ethanol and carbon dioxide.
ex: Ethanol is made from glucose by {{fermentation}}.

def: Containing a large amount of solute relative to solvent.
ex: Handle {{concentrated}} sulfuric acid with great care.

def: Containing a small amount of solute relative to solvent.
ex: Add water to make a more {{dilute}} solution.

def: Describing a substance that contains no water of crystallisation.
ex: {{Anhydrous}} copper sulfate is white until water is added.

def: Describing a compound that contains water of crystallisation.
ex: {{Hydrated}} copper sulfate forms blue crystals.

def: Thermodynamic quantity representing total heat content of a system at constant pressure.
ex: The change in {{enthalpy}} tells us whether a reaction releases or absorbs heat.

def: Measure of disorder or randomness of particles within a thermodynamic system.
ex: The {{entropy}} of a gas is greater than that of the corresponding solid.

def: Energy available to do useful work, predicting reaction spontaneity at constant temperature.
ex: A negative {{Gibbs free energy}} indicates that a reaction proceeds spontaneously.

def: Amount of heat absorbed or released during a chemical reaction.
ex: The {{heat of reaction}} is measured under constant pressure conditions.

def: Energy required to break one mole of a particular covalent bond.
ex: The {{bond enthalpy}} of a carbonвАУcarbon double bond exceeds that of a single bond.

def: Measurement of heat changes accompanying chemical reactions or physical processes.
ex: Using {{calorimetry}}, we determined the energy released by the combustion.

def: Ratio of product to reactant concentrations at any point during a reaction.
ex: Comparing the {{reaction quotient}} with the equilibrium constant predicts the reaction direction.

def: Ratio quantifying reactant and product concentrations at chemical equilibrium.
ex: A large {{equilibrium constant}} means the products are strongly favoured.

def: System at equilibrium shifts to counteract any imposed change in conditions.
ex: By {{Le Chatelier's principle}}, adding reactant drives the equilibrium towards products.

def: Proportionality factor linking reaction rate to reactant concentrations in a rate law.
ex: The {{rate constant}} increases sharply as temperature rises.

def: Sum of exponents of concentration terms in the experimental rate law.
ex: The {{order of reaction}} is determined experimentally, not from the equation.

def: Model explaining reaction rates through effective collisions between reacting particles.
ex: According to {{collision theory}}, particles must collide with sufficient energy to react.

def: Pressure exerted by a vapour in equilibrium with its liquid.
ex: A liquid boils when its {{vapour pressure}} equals atmospheric pressure.

def: Rise in boiling point when a solute dissolves in a solvent.
ex: Adding salt causes {{boiling point elevation}} in water.

def: Property depending only on the number of solute particles present.
ex: Osmotic pressure is a {{colligative property}} of dilute solutions.

def: Pressure needed to stop solvent flow across a semipermeable membrane.
ex: The {{osmotic pressure}} rises with increasing solute concentration.

def: Equation relating pressure, volume, temperature and amount of an ideal gas.
ex: The {{ideal gas law}} combines Boyle's, Charles's and Avogadro's laws.

def: Pressure a single gas would exert if occupying the container alone.
ex: The {{partial pressure}} of oxygen determines its transfer into the blood.

def: Enthalpy change measured under standard conditions of pressure and temperature.
ex: The {{standard enthalpy}} of formation is defined for one mole of compound.

def: Heat needed to raise one kilogram of substance by one kelvin.
ex: Water has a high {{specific heat capacity}}, resisting rapid temperature changes.

def: Total kinetic and potential energy of all particles within a system.
ex: The {{internal energy}} changes when heat or work crosses the boundary.

def: Energy is conserved; it cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred.
ex: The {{first law of thermodynamics}} relates internal energy to heat and work.

def: Total entropy of an isolated system always increases over time.
ex: The {{second law of thermodynamics}} explains why heat flows spontaneously to cooler bodies.

def: Total enthalpy change is independent of the reaction pathway taken.
ex: Using {{Hess's law}}, we calculate enthalpies that cannot be measured directly.

def: Enthalpy change when one mole of compound forms from its elements.
ex: The {{enthalpy of formation}} of an element in its standard state is zero.

def: Enthalpy change when one mole of substance burns completely in oxygen.
ex: The {{enthalpy of combustion}} of methane is strongly exothermic.

def: Energy released when gaseous ions combine to form an ionic solid.
ex: A high {{lattice energy}} makes an ionic compound very stable.

def: Thermochemical cycle used to calculate lattice energy of ionic compounds.
ex: The {{BornвАУHaber cycle}} applies Hess's law to ionic bonding.

def: Reaction that proceeds without continuous external input of energy.
ex: A {{spontaneous reaction}} has a negative Gibbs free energy change.

def: Difference in Gibbs free energy between products and reactants.
ex: The {{free energy change}} determines whether a process is feasible.

def: Equation expressing reaction rate in terms of reactant concentrations.
ex: The {{rate law}} must be determined by experiment.

def: Slowest step in a mechanism that controls the overall reaction rate.
ex: The {{rate-determining step}} involves breaking the strongest bond.

def: Sequence of elementary steps by which reactants convert into products.
ex: The proposed {{reaction mechanism}} explains the observed rate law.

def: Species formed and consumed during a multi-step reaction mechanism.
ex: The reactive {{intermediate}} does not appear in the overall equation.

def: Equation relating rate constant to temperature and activation energy.
ex: The {{Arrhenius equation}} shows rate constants rising exponentially with temperature.

def: Constant in the Arrhenius equation related to collision frequency and orientation.
ex: The {{frequency factor}} reflects how often correctly oriented collisions occur.

def: Highest-energy arrangement of atoms along the reaction pathway.
ex: The {{transition state}} exists at the peak of the energy profile.

def: State where forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates.
ex: At {{dynamic equilibrium}}, concentrations remain constant though reactions continue.

def: Reaction proceeding from reactants towards products in a reversible system.
ex: Heating the flask favoured the endothermic {{forward reaction}}.

def: Reaction converting products back into reactants in a reversible process.
ex: Removing product slows the {{backward reaction}}.

def: Ratio of moles of one component to total moles present.
ex: The {{mole fraction}} of nitrogen in air is about 0.78.

def: Concentration expressed as moles of solute per kilogram of solvent.
ex: Unlike molarity, {{molality}} does not change with temperature.

def: Concentration expressed as moles of solute per litre of solution.
ex: The {{molarity}} of the acid was found by titration.

def: Lowering of freezing point when a solute dissolves in a solvent.
ex: Salt causes {{freezing point depression}}, melting ice on roads.

def: Vapour pressure of a solvent is proportional to its mole fraction.
ex: An ideal solution obeys {{Raoult's law}} across all compositions.

def: Gas solubility in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure.
ex: {{Henry's law}} explains why fizzy drinks release gas when opened.

def: Total pressure equals the sum of the partial pressures present.
ex: By {{Dalton's law}}, each gas contributes independently to total pressure.

def: At constant temperature, gas pressure is inversely proportional to volume.
ex: {{Boyle's law}} describes how a syringe compresses trapped air.

def: At constant pressure, gas volume is proportional to absolute temperature.
ex: {{Charles's law}} explains why a balloon expands when warmed.

def: Equal volumes of gases contain equal numbers of molecules under identical conditions.
ex: {{Avogadro's law}} links gas volume directly to amount of substance.

def: Universal proportionality constant appearing in the ideal gas equation.
ex: The {{gas constant}} equals 8.31 joules per mole per kelvin.

def: Lowest possible temperature at which particle motion theoretically ceases.
ex: {{Absolute zero}} corresponds to zero kelvin, or вИТ273.15 degrees Celsius.

def: Model describing matter as particles in constant random motion.
ex: The {{kinetic theory}} explains gas pressure through molecular collisions.

def: Square root of the mean of squared molecular speeds in a gas.
ex: The {{root mean square speed}} increases with the square root of temperature.

def: Curve showing the spread of molecular speeds in a gas sample.
ex: Raising temperature broadens and shifts the {{MaxwellвАУBoltzmann distribution}} rightwards.

def: Gas whose behaviour deviates from ideal predictions at high pressures.
ex: A {{real gas}} shows attractive forces that an ideal gas ignores.

def: Ratio measuring how far a real gas departs from ideal behaviour.
ex: A {{compressibility factor}} of one indicates ideal gas behaviour.

def: Modified gas equation accounting for molecular volume and intermolecular attraction.
ex: The {{van der Waals equation}} corrects the ideal gas law for real gases.

def: Reference condition of a substance used for thermodynamic measurements.
ex: Enthalpy values are quoted relative to each element's {{standard state}}.

def: Agreed reference temperature and pressure for reporting measurements.
ex: All enthalpies were measured under {{standard conditions}}.

def: Reaction that releases free energy and proceeds spontaneously.
ex: An {{exergonic reaction}} has a negative free energy change.

def: Reaction that absorbs free energy and is non-spontaneous.
ex: An {{endergonic reaction}} requires coupling to an energy source.

def: Property depending only on current state, not the path taken.
ex: Enthalpy is a {{state function}}, so Hess's law applies.

def: Process occurring without any heat exchange with the surroundings.
ex: In an {{adiabatic process}}, compressing a gas raises its temperature.

def: Process taking place at constant temperature throughout.
ex: During an {{isothermal process}}, heat flows to keep temperature fixed.

def: Enthalpy change when one mole of solute dissolves completely.
ex: The {{enthalpy of solution}} of ammonium nitrate is endothermic.

def: Enthalpy change when acid and base react to form water.
ex: The {{enthalpy of neutralisation}} of strong acids and bases is nearly constant.

def: Enthalpy change when gaseous ions become surrounded by water molecules.
ex: A large {{enthalpy of hydration}} favours dissolving of an ionic solid.

def: Sealed vessel measuring heat released by combustion at constant volume.
ex: A {{bomb calorimeter}} determines the energy content of fuels.

def: Study of heat energy associated with chemical reactions and changes.
ex: {{Thermochemistry}} allows chemists to predict energy released by fuels.

def: Solution resisting pH change when small amounts of acid are added.
ex: A {{buffer solution}} keeps blood pH within a narrow range.

def: Reduced solubility of a salt caused by a shared ion.
ex: The {{common ion effect}} suppresses ionisation of a weak acid.

def: Equilibrium constant describing dissolution of a sparingly soluble salt.
ex: Precipitation occurs once the ionic product exceeds the {{solubility product}}.

def: Equilibrium constant measuring the extent of acid ionisation in water.
ex: A strong acid has a very large {{acid dissociation constant}}.

def: Fraction of molecules that dissociate into ions in solution.
ex: The {{degree of dissociation}} of a weak acid increases on dilution.

def: Product of hydrogen and hydroxide ion concentrations in pure water.
ex: The {{ionic product of water}} equals 1√Ч10вБї¬євБі at room temperature.

def: Heat required to raise a body's temperature by one kelvin.
ex: The {{heat capacity}} of a substance depends on its mass.

def: Heat absorbed or released during a change of physical state.
ex: The {{latent heat}} of vaporisation converts boiling water into steam.

def: Process releasing heat energy to the surrounding environment.
ex: Condensation is an {{exothermic process}} that warms the surroundings.

def: Process absorbing heat energy from the surrounding environment.
ex: Melting ice is an {{endothermic process}} that cools its surroundings.

def: Diagram showing energy changes as a reaction progresses to products.
ex: The {{energy profile}} shows the activation energy as a peak.

def: State where two bodies in contact reach the same temperature.
ex: Objects at {{thermal equilibrium}} exchange no net heat.

def: Portion of the universe under study in thermodynamics.
ex: An isolated {{system}} exchanges neither matter nor energy with surroundings.

def: Everything outside the system that can exchange energy with it.
ex: An exothermic reaction transfers heat to the {{surroundings}}.

def: Energy transferred when a force moves through a distance.
ex: An expanding gas does {{work}} against external pressure.

def: Unstable, high-energy species existing momentarily at the transition state.
ex: The {{activated complex}} decomposes to form either products or reactants.

def: Collision with enough energy and correct orientation to cause reaction.
ex: Only an {{effective collision}} leads to product formation.

def: Heat needed to raise one mole of substance by one kelvin.
ex: The {{molar heat capacity}} of a gas differs at constant pressure and volume.

def: Heat absorbed when one mole of solid melts into liquid.
ex: The {{heat of fusion}} of ice is absorbed at zero degrees.

def: Heat absorbed when one mole of liquid becomes vapour.
ex: The {{heat of vaporisation}} of water is unusually high.

def: Ratio of a solute's concentrations between two immiscible solvents.
ex: The {{partition coefficient}} governs how a drug distributes in the body.

def: Condition where intermediate concentrations remain constant during a reaction.
ex: The {{steady state}} approximation simplifies complex kinetic analysis.

def: Enthalpy difference between reactants and the transition state.
ex: A high {{activation enthalpy}} corresponds to a slow reaction.

def: Volume occupied by one mole of a gas under stated conditions.
ex: The {{molar volume}} of an ideal gas is 24 litres at room conditions.

def: Factor by which reaction rate increases per ten-degree temperature rise.
ex: Many reactions have a {{temperature coefficient}} near two.

def: Independent way in which a molecule can store energy.
ex: A diatomic molecule has translational and rotational {{degree of freedom}} contributions.

def: Temperature above which a gas cannot be liquefied by pressure.
ex: Above its {{critical temperature}}, carbon dioxide stays gaseous under any pressure.

def: Graph showing the physical states of a substance versus conditions.
ex: The {{phase diagram}} marks the triple point where all states coexist.

def: Conditions where solid, liquid and gas phases coexist in equilibrium.
ex: At the {{triple point}}, water exists simultaneously as ice, liquid and vapour.

def: Entropy of one mole of substance under standard conditions.
ex: Gases generally have a higher {{standard entropy}} than solids.

def: Free energy change when one mole of compound forms from elements.
ex: A negative {{free energy of formation}} indicates a thermodynamically stable compound.

def: Force exerted by particles per unit area of container walls.
ex: Increasing the {{pressure}} shifts a gaseous equilibrium towards fewer molecules.

def: Heat released when an acid reacts completely with a base.
ex: The {{heat of neutralisation}} is exothermic for strong acidвАУbase pairs.

def: Energy a particle possesses due to its motion.
ex: Raising temperature increases the average {{kinetic energy}} of molecules.

def: Stored energy arising from the positions and bonds of particles.
ex: Breaking bonds converts chemical {{potential energy}} into heat.

def: Enthalpy change forming one mole of gaseous atoms from an element.
ex: The {{enthalpy of atomisation}} contributes to the BornвАУHaber cycle.

def: Energy to remove one electron from each gaseous atom.
ex: The {{first ionisation enthalpy}} generally increases across a period.

def: Energy change when a gaseous atom gains one electron.
ex: Chlorine has a highly exothermic {{electron affinity}}.

def: Concentration of a species once a reaction reaches equilibrium.
ex: The {{equilibrium concentration}} of product is found from the constant.

def: The chemical substance whose amount or identity is being measured
ex: The {{analyte}} was extracted from the sample before measurement.

def: Study of how matter interacts with light across different wavelengths
ex: {{Spectroscopy}} reveals the electronic structure of the atoms involved.

def: An instrument that measures how much light a solution absorbs
ex: She placed the cuvette inside the {{spectrophotometer}} for reading.

def: A measure of how much light a substance absorbs at a wavelength
ex: The {{absorbance}} increased as the dye concentration rose.

def: The fraction of light that passes through a sample solution
ex: High {{transmittance}} means very little light was absorbed.

def: The distance between two successive peaks of a light wave
ex: Each pigment absorbs light at a characteristic {{wavelength}}.

def: A graph relating signal to known concentrations of a standard
ex: We read the unknown concentration off the {{calibration curve}}.

def: A solution of precisely known concentration used in analysis
ex: A {{standard solution}} of known molarity calibrated the instrument.

def: The stage in titration where the indicator signals reaction completion
ex: The pink colour marked the {{endpoint}} of the titration.

def: Where added titrant exactly neutralises the analyte in solution
ex: At the {{equivalence point}} the moles of acid equalled the base.

def: Quantitative method based on measuring the mass of a product
ex: {{Gravimetric analysis}} determined the sulfate content by weighing.

def: Quantitative method based on measuring reacting volumes of solution
ex: {{Volumetric analysis}} uses titration to find concentration.

def: Identifying which substances are present in a sample
ex: {{Qualitative analysis}} confirmed the presence of chloride ions.

def: Measuring how much of a substance is present in a sample
ex: {{Quantitative analysis}} showed the ore contained ten percent iron.

def: Technique that identifies molecules by their mass-to-charge ratio
ex: {{Mass spectrometry}} confirmed the molecular formula of the drug.

def: A plot of ion abundance against mass-to-charge ratio
ex: The {{mass spectrum}} showed a molecular ion peak at 78.

def: The ion formed when a whole molecule loses one electron
ex: The {{molecular ion}} peak gives the compound's relative molecular mass.

def: The tallest peak in a mass spectrum, taken as full intensity
ex: The {{base peak}} represents the most abundant fragment ion.

def: Method identifying bonds by their infrared absorption frequencies
ex: {{Infrared spectroscopy}} revealed a strong carbonyl absorption.

def: Technique probing atomic nuclei in a strong magnetic field
ex: {{Nuclear magnetic resonance}} mapped the hydrogen environments.

def: Position of an NMR signal reflecting a nucleus's environment
ex: The {{chemical shift}} at 7 ppm indicated aromatic protons.

def: Separating volatile compounds carried by a gas through a column
ex: {{Gas chromatography}} separated the mixture of hydrocarbons.

def: Separating dissolved compounds pumped under pressure through a column
ex: {{High-performance liquid chromatography}} resolved the drug metabolites.

def: Time a compound takes to travel through a chromatography column
ex: Each solvent had a distinct {{retention time}} on the chromatogram.

def: A recorded plot of detector signal against elution time
ex: The {{chromatogram}} showed three well-separated peaks.

def: The fluid that carries the sample through a chromatography column
ex: The {{mobile phase}} was a mixture of water and methanol.

def: The fixed material in a column that retards sample components
ex: Polar compounds cling longer to the {{stationary phase}}.

def: The solvent used to carry analytes through a column
ex: Changing the {{eluent}} altered the separation of the pigments.

def: The process of washing components out of a column
ex: Rapid {{elution}} produced sharp, narrow peaks.

def: The leading edge of solvent as it moves up a plate
ex: We marked the {{solvent front}} before the plate dried.

def: Ratio of distances travelled by a spot and the solvent
ex: Each compound has a characteristic {{retention factor}} value.

def: Light of specific wavelengths emitted by excited atoms
ex: Sodium's {{emission spectrum}} shows a bright yellow line.

def: Pattern of wavelengths absorbed by a substance from light
ex: The dye's {{absorption spectrum}} peaked in the blue region.

def: A sharp line in a spectrum at a specific wavelength
ex: Each {{spectral line}} corresponds to a defined energy transition.

def: A small transparent cell holding liquid for optical measurement
ex: Fill the {{cuvette}} to the mark before taking a reading.

def: A device that selects a single wavelength from mixed light
ex: The {{monochromator}} isolated the 540-nanometre beam.

def: Absorbance is proportional to concentration and path length
ex: The {{Beer-Lambert law}} links absorbance to solution concentration.

def: Distance light travels through a sample in the cell
ex: A standard cuvette gives a {{path length}} of one centimetre.

def: Lowest amount of analyte that can be reliably detected
ex: The method's {{detection limit}} was one part per billion.

def: A reference sample without analyte used to zero instruments
ex: We zeroed the reading using a solvent {{blank}}.

def: A measured portion taken from a larger sample
ex: A ten-millilitre {{aliquot}} was pipetted for titration.

def: A tube for delivering an accurate volume of liquid
ex: Use a {{pipette}} to transfer exactly twenty-five millilitres.

def: A graduated tube delivering variable volumes during titration
ex: Read the {{burette}} at eye level to avoid parallax.

def: A flask calibrated to hold one exact volume
ex: Dilute to the mark in a {{volumetric flask}}.

def: The curved surface of liquid in a narrow tube
ex: Align the bottom of the {{meniscus}} with the graduation.

def: The solution of known concentration added during a titration
ex: The {{titrant}} was added slowly until the colour changed.

def: Determining the exact concentration of a solution before use
ex: {{Standardisation}} of the alkali used a primary standard.

def: A pure, stable substance used to prepare exact concentrations
ex: Anhydrous sodium carbonate is a common {{primary standard}}.

def: A unit expressing very small concentrations in a mixture
ex: Fluoride was present at two {{parts per million}}.

def: Making stepwise dilutions to obtain a range of concentrations
ex: A {{serial dilution}} produced standards for the calibration curve.

def: A mixture that shows a colour range across the pH scale
ex: {{Universal indicator}} turned green in the neutral solution.

def: A dye that turns red in acid and blue in alkali
ex: Blue {{litmus}} paper turned red in the acidic sample.

def: An indicator colourless in acid and pink in alkali
ex: {{Phenolphthalein}} turned faint pink at the endpoint.

def: An indicator changing from red to yellow across acidic pH
ex: {{Methyl orange}} suits titrations with a strong acid.

def: An electronic instrument measuring the acidity of a solution
ex: The {{pH meter}} read 4.5 for the vinegar sample.

def: Measuring metal concentration from light emitted in a flame
ex: {{Flame photometry}} quantified the potassium in the water.

def: Determining concentration from the intensity of a solution's colour
ex: {{Colorimetry}} estimated the iron content from the colour.

def: Identifying substances by studying their characteristic spectra
ex: {{Spectral analysis}} confirmed the metal present in the alloy.

def: A well-characterised material used to compare measurements against
ex: Results were validated against a certified {{reference standard}}.

def: A known compound added to correct for measurement variation
ex: An {{internal standard}} corrected for injection differences.

def: How close a measured value is to the true value
ex: The method's {{accuracy}} was checked against a known sample.

def: How closely repeated measurements agree with one another
ex: Good {{precision}} means repeated readings cluster tightly.

def: How strongly an instrument responds to a change in analyte
ex: Higher {{sensitivity}} lets the detector see tiny amounts.

def: The ability to distinguish two closely spaced peaks or signals
ex: Better {{resolution}} separated the two overlapping peaks.

def: Steps that ready a sample for reliable instrumental analysis
ex: Careful {{sample preparation}} removed interfering particles.

def: Separating a compound by transferring it into a solvent
ex: Solvent {{extraction}} isolated the caffeine from the tea.

def: Breaking a sample down with heat and acid before analysis
ex: Acid {{digestion}} dissolved the soil for metal testing.

def: Burning off organic matter to leave inorganic residue
ex: {{Ashing}} the food sample left mineral residue to weigh.

def: A sealed container keeping samples dry with a drying agent
ex: Cool the crucible in a {{desiccator}} before weighing.

def: A heat-resistant vessel for strongly heating solids
ex: The residue was ignited in a porcelain {{crucible}}.

def: A steady weight reached after repeated heating and cooling
ex: Heat the sample until it reaches {{constant mass}}.

def: Measuring concentration from light scattered by suspended particles
ex: {{Nephelometry}} assessed the turbidity of the water sample.

def: Measuring analyte concentration from a cell's electrode potential
ex: {{Potentiometry}} tracked the voltage during the titration.

def: A measure of how well a solution conducts electric current
ex: The {{conductivity}} rose as more ions dissolved.

def: Cloudiness of a liquid caused by suspended fine particles
ex: High {{turbidity}} scattered the light passing through.

def: A reaction in which one atom or group replaces another in a molecule.
ex: A {{substitution reaction}} swaps a hydrogen atom for a halogen.

def: A reaction where atoms add across a double or triple bond.
ex: An {{addition reaction}} converts an alkene into a saturated product.

def: A reaction that removes atoms to form a double bond.
ex: An {{elimination reaction}} produces an alkene from a haloalkane.

def: A reaction joining molecules while releasing a small molecule like water.
ex: A {{condensation reaction}} links two amino acids and expels water.

def: An electron-rich species that donates a pair to form a bond.
ex: A {{nucleophile}} attacks the electron-poor carbon centre.

def: An electron-poor species that accepts a pair of electrons.
ex: An {{electrophile}} is attacked by an electron-rich nucleophile.

def: A highly reactive species with an unpaired electron.
ex: A {{free radical}} initiates the chain reaction of chlorination.

def: A positively charged carbon intermediate formed during many reactions.
ex: A stable {{carbocation}} forms as an intermediate in the mechanism.

def: A negatively charged carbon intermediate with a lone electron pair.
ex: A {{carbanion}} behaves as a strong nucleophile in reactions.

def: A stable ring compound with delocalised electrons, such as benzene.
ex: An {{aromatic compound}} resists addition but favours substitution.

def: An organic compound with open or non-aromatic chain structures.
ex: An {{aliphatic compound}} has a straight or branched carbon chain.

def: A six-carbon aromatic ring with delocalised pi electrons.
ex: The {{benzene ring}} gives the molecule great chemical stability.

def: An organic compound with an oxygen atom linking two carbon groups.
ex: An {{ether}} contains a carbon-oxygen-carbon linkage.

def: An organic compound derived from ammonia with a nitrogen group.
ex: An {{amine}} acts as a weak base in solution.

def: A compound with a carbonyl group bonded to nitrogen.
ex: An {{amide}} bond links the residues in a protein.

def: A reaction that introduces a halogen atom into a molecule.
ex: The {{halogenation}} of methane needs ultraviolet light.

def: The addition of hydrogen across a double or triple bond.
ex: The {{hydrogenation}} of an alkene yields an alkane.

def: A reaction forming an ester from an acid and an alcohol.
ex: The {{esterification}} of ethanol produces a sweet-smelling ester.

def: The hydrolysis of a fat with alkali to make soap.
ex: The {{saponification}} of a fat produces soap and glycerol.

def: A reaction that removes water from a molecule.
ex: The {{dehydration}} of an alcohol gives an alkene.

def: The addition of water across a double bond.
ex: The {{hydration}} of ethene forms ethanol.

def: A reaction introducing a nitro group into an aromatic ring.
ex: The {{nitration}} of benzene uses a mixture of strong acids.

def: A reaction introducing a sulfonic acid group into a molecule.
ex: The {{sulfonation}} of benzene attaches a sulfonic acid group.

def: A reaction adding an alkyl group to a molecule.
ex: The {{alkylation}} of benzene introduces an alkyl side chain.

def: A number showing the electron loss or gain of an atom.
ex: Carbon changes its {{oxidation state}} during the reaction.

def: A self-sustaining reaction where each step generates the next.
ex: A {{chain reaction}} propagates through radical intermediates.

def: The first step that generates reactive radicals in a chain.
ex: The {{initiation}} step forms radicals when bonds break homolytically.

def: The repeating steps that continue a radical chain reaction.
ex: During {{propagation}}, radicals react and regenerate to sustain the chain.

def: The step that ends a chain by combining two radicals.
ex: The {{termination}} step joins two radicals into one molecule.

def: The process of separating atoms by cleaving a chemical bond.
ex: {{Bond breaking}} absorbs energy during the reaction.

def: The process of joining atoms by creating a chemical bond.
ex: {{Bond formation}} releases energy as new bonds appear.

def: Bond breaking where each atom keeps one shared electron.
ex: {{Homolytic fission}} of a bond produces two radicals.

def: Bond breaking where one atom keeps both shared electrons.
ex: {{Heterolytic fission}} produces a cation and an anion.

def: An atom or group that departs with a bonding pair.
ex: A good {{leaving group}} accepts the electron pair easily.

def: The molecule that undergoes change during a reaction.
ex: The {{substrate}} is attacked by the incoming nucleophile.

def: A hydrocarbon with only single bonds between carbon atoms.
ex: A {{saturated hydrocarbon}} contains the maximum number of hydrogens.

def: A hydrocarbon containing one or more carbon double or triple bonds.
ex: An {{unsaturated hydrocarbon}} decolourises bromine water.

def: A covalent bond sharing two pairs of electrons.
ex: The {{double bond}} in ethene makes it reactive.

def: A covalent bond sharing three pairs of electrons.
ex: The {{triple bond}} in ethyne holds six shared electrons.

def: A covalent bond sharing one pair of electrons.
ex: Each {{single bond}} in ethane joins two atoms.

def: A bond formed by sideways overlap of p orbitals.
ex: The {{pi bond}} breaks first during an addition reaction.

def: A strong bond formed by head-on overlap of orbitals.
ex: A {{sigma bond}} lies directly between two nuclei.

def: The spreading of electrons over several atoms in a molecule.
ex: {{Delocalisation}} of electrons stabilises the benzene ring.

def: The representation of a molecule by several contributing structures.
ex: {{Resonance}} explains the equal bond lengths in benzene.

def: One of two isomers that interconvert by moving a hydrogen.
ex: The keto {{tautomer}} predominates over the enol form.

def: The property of a molecule not superimposable on its mirror image.
ex: {{Chirality}} arises from a carbon with four different groups.

def: One of a pair of non-superimposable mirror-image molecules.
ex: Each {{enantiomer}} rotates plane-polarised light oppositely.

def: An isomer differing only in the spatial arrangement of atoms.
ex: A {{stereoisomer}} has the same bonds but different geometry.

def: An isomer with a different functional group from another.
ex: An alcohol and its {{functional isomer}} share a formula.

def: An isomer with the same formula but different atom connectivity.
ex: A {{structural isomer}} has atoms joined in a different order.

def: A formula showing how atoms are bonded within a molecule.
ex: The {{structural formula}} reveals each bond in the compound.

def: A formula giving the actual number of each atom present.
ex: The {{molecular formula}} of ethanol is written concisely.

def: A formula showing the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms.
ex: The {{empirical formula}} gives the simplest atomic ratio.

def: A formula representing every member of a homologous series.
ex: The {{general formula}} for alkanes fits every member.

def: A formula showing all atoms and bonds drawn out fully.
ex: The {{displayed formula}} shows every atom and every bond.

def: Changing one functional group into another through a reaction.
ex: {{Functional group interconversion}} turns an alcohol into a halide.

def: Substitution where a nucleophile replaces a leaving group.
ex: {{Nucleophilic substitution}} converts a haloalkane into an alcohol.

def: Addition where an electrophile attacks an electron-rich double bond.
ex: {{Electrophilic addition}} adds bromine across an alkene.

def: Substitution where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen on a ring.
ex: {{Electrophilic substitution}} is typical of aromatic compounds.

def: Substitution proceeding through reactive radical intermediates.
ex: {{Radical substitution}} explains the chlorination of alkanes.

def: A rule predicting where hydrogen adds during an addition reaction.
ex: {{Markovnikov's rule}} predicts the major addition product.

def: The energising of molecules so that a reaction can proceed.
ex: {{Activation}} of the reactants is needed to begin bonding.

def: A reaction in which parts of two compounds swap places.
ex: An {{exchange reaction}} trades groups between two molecules.

def: An isomer differing in the branching of the carbon chain.
ex: A {{chain isomer}} has a differently branched carbon skeleton.

def: An isomer differing in the position of a functional group.
ex: A {{position isomer}} moves the functional group along the chain.

def: A substance that generates radicals to start a chain reaction.
ex: A {{radical initiator}} decomposes to produce the first radicals.

def: A group derived from a carboxylic acid by removing hydroxyl.
ex: An {{acyl group}} transfers during many condensation reactions.

def: A group formed by removing one hydrogen from an alkane.
ex: An {{alkyl group}} acts as a substituent on the ring.

def: A functional group with a carbon double-bonded to oxygen.
ex: The {{carbonyl group}} defines both aldehydes and ketones.

def: A functional group containing nitrogen bonded to hydrogen atoms.
ex: The {{amino group}} makes the molecule behave as a base.

def: A functional group made of nitrogen bonded to two oxygens.
ex: The {{nitro group}} is added during aromatic nitration.

def: Burning in plenty of oxygen to give carbon dioxide and water.
ex: {{Complete combustion}} of a fuel yields carbon dioxide and water.

def: Burning in limited oxygen, forming carbon monoxide or soot.
ex: {{Incomplete combustion}} of a fuel produces toxic carbon monoxide.

def: Heating a mixture while continuously condensing and returning vapour.
ex: The reaction is heated under {{reflux}} to drive completion.

def: A polymer formed by monomers adding across double bonds.
ex: An {{addition polymer}} forms when alkene monomers join together.

def: A polymer formed with the loss of small molecules like water.
ex: A {{condensation polymer}} releases water as its chains grow.

def: The smallest recurring structure within a polymer chain.
ex: The {{repeat unit}} shows the basic pattern of the polymer.

def: A reaction that removes hydrogen from a molecule.
ex: {{Dehydrogenation}} of an alkane can form an alkene.

def: A reaction rearranging a molecule into one of its isomers.
ex: {{Isomerisation}} converts a straight chain into a branched one.

def: A process rearranging hydrocarbons into more useful branched or cyclic forms.
ex: {{Reforming}} turns straight-chain alkanes into ring compounds.

def: An atom or group replacing hydrogen on a parent chain.
ex: Each {{substituent}} is named and numbered along the chain.

def: A compound in which a central metal atom bonds to surrounding ligands via dative bonds.
ex: The blue colour indicates a copper {{coordination compound}} in the solution.

def: An ion or molecule that donates electron pairs to a central metal atom.
ex: Water can act as a {{ligand}} in many transition metal complexes.

def: A natural rock containing enough metal to be extracted profitably.
ex: Iron is extracted from its {{ore}} in a blast furnace.

def: Heating an ore with a reducing agent to extract the metal.
ex: Copper is obtained by {{smelting}} its sulfide ore at high temperature.

def: A tall furnace used to reduce iron ore into molten iron.
ex: Coke burns inside the {{blast furnace}} to reduce the iron oxide.

def: A mixture of a metal with other elements to improve properties.
ex: Steel is an {{alloy}} of iron and carbon.

def: The corrosion of iron forming hydrated iron oxide in moist air.
ex: {{Rusting}} occurs when iron is exposed to both water and oxygen.

def: Coating iron or steel with zinc to prevent corrosion.
ex: {{Galvanising}} protects the steel roof from rust for many years.

def: The raw material fed into an industrial chemical process.
ex: Naphtha is a common {{feedstock}} for the petrochemical industry.

def: An unrefined mixture of hydrocarbons pumped from underground reservoirs.
ex: {{Crude oil}} is separated into fractions by fractional distillation.

def: Separating a liquid mixture into fractions by their differing boiling points.
ex: {{Fractional distillation}} separates crude oil into petrol and diesel.

def: A tall tower where vapour and liquid separate mixtures by boiling point.
ex: Lighter fractions rise to the top of the {{distillation column}}.

def: A chemical substance derived from petroleum or natural gas.
ex: Plastics are made from a {{petrochemical}} obtained from crude oil.

def: A substance added to soil to supply nutrients for plant growth.
ex: Ammonium nitrate is a widely used nitrogen {{fertiliser}}.

def: An industrial method combining nitrogen and hydrogen to make ammonia.
ex: The {{Haber process}} produces ammonia under high pressure and temperature.

def: The industrial method for making sulfuric acid using a vanadium catalyst.
ex: The {{contact process}} converts sulfur dioxide into sulfur trioxide.

def: The industrial reaction forming ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen gases.
ex: {{Ammonia synthesis}} relies on an iron catalyst at high pressure.

def: A layer of solid catalyst through which reacting gases pass.
ex: The gas mixture flows over the {{catalyst bed}} to speed reaction.

def: A device where electric current drives a non-spontaneous chemical reaction.
ex: Aluminium is extracted inside a large {{electrolysis cell}}.

def: Depositing a thin metal layer onto an object using electrolysis.
ex: {{Electroplating}} coats cheap metal with a shiny layer of silver.

def: Preventing corrosion by attaching a more reactive metal that corrodes instead.
ex: Zinc blocks give {{sacrificial protection}} to steel ship hulls.

def: Calcium oxide made by heating limestone in a kiln.
ex: {{Quicklime}} reacts vigorously with water to form slaked lime.

def: Calcium hydroxide formed when quicklime reacts with water.
ex: Farmers spread {{slaked lime}} to reduce soil acidity.

def: A sedimentary rock made mostly of calcium carbonate.
ex: {{Limestone}} is added to the blast furnace to remove impurities.

def: Breaking a compound into simpler substances using heat energy.
ex: {{Thermal decomposition}} of limestone releases carbon dioxide gas.

def: The waste material that floats on molten metal during smelting.
ex: Molten {{slag}} is tapped off separately from the liquid iron.

def: A substance added to remove impurities during metal extraction or joining.
ex: Limestone acts as a {{flux}} that combines with sandy impurities.

def: A list ranking metals by their tendency to react and displace others.
ex: Potassium sits near the top of the {{reactivity series}}.

def: Thickening the oxide layer on aluminium using electrolysis for protection.
ex: {{Anodising}} makes the aluminium frame more resistant to corrosion.

def: A central metal ion bonded to several ligands carrying an overall charge.
ex: The deep blue {{complex ion}} forms when ammonia is added to copper.

def: A complex where a ligand bonds to a metal at several points.
ex: EDTA forms a stable {{chelate}} with calcium ions.

def: The number of ligand atoms directly bonded to a central metal.
ex: An octahedral complex usually has a {{coordination number}} of six.

def: Able to react with both acids and bases as an oxide or hydroxide.
ex: Aluminium oxide is {{amphoteric}} and dissolves in acid or alkali.

def: An oxide that forms an acid or base when combined with water.
ex: Sulfur trioxide is the {{anhydride}} of sulfuric acid.

def: A concentrated solution of sodium chloride in water.
ex: Electrolysis of {{brine}} produces chlorine, hydrogen and sodium hydroxide.

def: Electrolysis of brine to make chlorine, hydrogen and sodium hydroxide.
ex: The {{chlor-alkali process}} supplies chlorine for water treatment.

def: Removing sulfur compounds from fuels or flue gases to cut pollution.
ex: Flue gas {{desulfurisation}} reduces sulfur dioxide from power stations.

def: Reacting hydrocarbons with steam to produce hydrogen and carbon monoxide.
ex: {{Steam reforming}} of methane is a major source of hydrogen.

def: A device that converts harmful exhaust gases into less toxic ones.
ex: The {{catalytic converter}} changes carbon monoxide into carbon dioxide.

def: An industrial plant where crude oil is processed into useful products.
ex: The {{refinery}} converts crude oil into petrol and lubricants.

def: A group of hydrocarbons collected together within a boiling range.
ex: The petrol {{fraction}} boils at a lower temperature than diesel.

def: A thick black residue from crude oil used for road surfacing.
ex: {{Bitumen}} is the heaviest fraction left at the column base.

def: A light oil fraction used as feedstock for chemicals and petrol.
ex: {{Naphtha}} is cracked to produce ethene for making plastics.

def: A middle oil fraction used mainly as aircraft and heating fuel.
ex: Jet engines burn {{kerosene}} refined from crude oil.

def: To lose shine as a metal surface reacts slowly with air.
ex: Silver spoons {{tarnish}} because sulfur compounds attack the surface.

def: Forming a thin protective oxide film that stops further corrosion.
ex: {{Passivation}} gives stainless steel its resistance to rust.

def: A chromium-containing alloy of iron that resists corrosion and staining.
ex: Cutlery is often made from {{stainless steel}} to prevent rust.

def: The impure iron produced directly from a blast furnace.
ex: {{Pig iron}} is brittle because it contains too much carbon.

def: The main ore of aluminium, rich in aluminium oxide.
ex: Aluminium is extracted from {{bauxite}} by electrolysis.

def: A mineral used to lower the melting point of aluminium oxide.
ex: Molten {{cryolite}} dissolves alumina during aluminium extraction.

def: Extracting a pure metal from solution using an electric current.
ex: {{Electrowinning}} deposits pure copper onto the cathode.

def: Dissolving a metal out of its ore using a liquid solvent.
ex: Gold is recovered by {{leaching}} the crushed ore with cyanide.

def: Heating an ore strongly in air to convert it to oxide.
ex: {{Roasting}} zinc sulfide in air produces zinc oxide.

def: Fusing powdered material into a solid mass using heat below melting.
ex: Iron ore fines undergo {{sintering}} before entering the furnace.

def: A measure of a species' tendency to gain electrons and be reduced.
ex: A high {{reduction potential}} means the ion is easily reduced.

def: A list of elements arranged by their standard electrode potentials.
ex: The {{electrochemical series}} predicts which metal displaces another.

def: A cell that generates electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction.
ex: A {{galvanic cell}} converts chemical energy into electrical energy.

def: The study of chemical reactions that involve electricity and electron transfer.
ex: Batteries and electrolysis both belong to {{electrochemistry}}.

def: Equipment that removes pollutant gases from an industrial exhaust stream.
ex: A {{scrubber}} removes acidic gases before they reach the chimney.

def: A furnace used for heating materials such as limestone or clay.
ex: Limestone is heated strongly in a rotary {{kiln}} to make lime.

def: To increase a process from laboratory size to industrial production.
ex: Engineers must {{scale up}} the reaction to a full plant.

def: A process where materials are added and products removed in fixed amounts.
ex: Fine chemicals are often made in a {{batch process}}.

def: A process where materials flow in and products flow out constantly.
ex: Ammonia is made using a {{continuous process}} for efficiency.

def: Waste gas released from an engine or industrial combustion process.
ex: The {{exhaust gas}} contains carbon dioxide and nitrogen oxides.

def: Rain made acidic by dissolved sulfur and nitrogen oxides.
ex: {{Acid rain}} damages forests and corrodes stone buildings.

def: Converting atmospheric nitrogen into compounds usable by living things.
ex: The Haber process is an industrial form of {{nitrogen fixation}}.

def: A solution of sulfur trioxide in concentrated sulfuric acid.
ex: Sulfur trioxide is dissolved to make {{oleum}} in the contact process.

def: A gaseous environment containing excess oxygen that promotes oxidation.
ex: Roasting occurs in an {{oxidising atmosphere}} rich in air.

def: Reheating hardened steel to reduce brittleness and improve toughness.
ex: {{Tempering}} makes the hardened blade less likely to snap.

def: Rapidly cooling hot metal in water or oil to harden it.
ex: {{Quenching}} the red-hot steel in water increases its hardness.

def: Accelerated corrosion when two different metals touch in an electrolyte.
ex: {{Galvanic corrosion}} eats away the less noble of two joined metals.

def: Reacting quicklime with water to produce calcium hydroxide.
ex: {{Slaking}} lime releases a large amount of heat.

def: The worthless rock and minerals surrounding a valuable ore.
ex: The {{gangue}} is separated from the ore before smelting begins.

def: Separating ore minerals from waste using air bubbles in water.
ex: {{Froth flotation}} concentrates copper ore before smelting.

def: The residue of impurities collected below the anode during refining.
ex: Precious metals are recovered from the {{anode mud}} in copper refining.

def: Vanadium(V) oxide used to speed the contact process for sulfuric acid.
ex: The {{vanadium catalyst}} converts sulfur dioxide to sulfur trioxide.

def: A catalyst in a different physical state from the reacting substances.
ex: Iron acts as a {{heterogeneous catalyst}} in ammonia production.

def: A catalyst in the same physical state as the reactants.
ex: An acid dissolved in solution can serve as a {{homogeneous catalyst}}.

def: The stress at which a metal begins to deform permanently.
ex: Beyond its {{yield point}} the steel bar bends without recovering.

def: Reprocessing used materials such as metals into new products.
ex: {{Recycling}} aluminium uses far less energy than extracting it.
